speaker: ROMEO
text: What, shall this speech be spoke for our excuse?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Or shall we on without apology?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: The date is out of such prolixity.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: We’ll have no Cupid hoodwinked with a scarf,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Bearing a Tartar’s painted bow of lath,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Scaring the ladies like a crowkeeper,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Nor no without-book prologue, faintly spoke

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: After the prompter, for our entrance.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: But let them measure us by what they will.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: We’ll measure them a measure and be gone.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Give me a torch. I am not for this ambling.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Being but heavy I will bear the light.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Nay, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Not I, believe me. You have dancing shoes

speaker: ROMEO
text: With nimble soles. I have a soul of lead

speaker: ROMEO
text: So stakes me to the ground I cannot move.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: You are a lover. Borrow Cupid’s wings

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And soar with them above a common bound.

speaker: ROMEO
text: I am too sore enpiercèd with his shaft

speaker: ROMEO
text: To soar with his light feathers, and so bound

speaker: ROMEO
text: I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Under love’s heavy burden do I sink.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And to sink in it should you burden love—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Too great oppression for a tender thing.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Is love a tender thing? It is too rough,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Too rude, too boist’rous, and it pricks like thorn.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: If love be rough with you, be rough with love.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Prick love for pricking, and you beat love down.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Give me a case to put my visage in.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: A visor for a visor. What care I

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: What curious eye doth cote deformities?

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Here are the beetle brows shall blush for me.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Come, knock and enter, and no sooner in

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: But every man betake him to his legs.

speaker: ROMEO
text: A torch for me. Let wantons light of heart

speaker: ROMEO
text: Tickle the senseless rushes with their heels,

speaker: ROMEO
text: For I am proverbed with a grandsire phrase:

speaker: ROMEO
text: I’ll be a candle holder and look on;

speaker: ROMEO
text: The game was ne’er so fair, and I am done.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Tut, dun’s the mouse, the constable’s own word.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: If thou art dun, we’ll draw thee from the mire—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Or, save your reverence, love—wherein thou stickest

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Up to the ears. Come, we burn daylight, ho!

speaker: ROMEO
text: Nay, that’s not so.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: I mean, sir, in delay

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: We waste our lights; in vain, light lights by day.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Five times in that ere once in our five wits.

speaker: ROMEO
text: And we mean well in going to this masque,

speaker: ROMEO
text: But ’tis no wit to go.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Why, may one ask?

speaker: ROMEO
text: I dreamt a dream tonight.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And so did I.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Well, what was yours?

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: That dreamers often lie.

speaker: ROMEO
text: In bed asleep while they do dream things true.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: O, then I see Queen Mab hath been with you.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: She is the fairies’ midwife, and she comes

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: In shape no bigger than an agate stone

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: On the forefinger of an alderman,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Drawn with a team of little atomi

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Over men’s noses as they lie asleep.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her wagon spokes made of long spinners’ legs,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: The cover of the wings of grasshoppers,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her traces of the smallest spider web,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her collars of the moonshine’s wat’ry beams,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her whip of cricket’s bone, the lash of film,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her wagoner a small gray-coated gnat,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Not half so big as a round little worm

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Pricked from the lazy finger of a maid.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Her chariot is an empty hazelnut,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Made by the joiner squirrel or old grub,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Time out o’ mind the fairies’ coachmakers.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And in this state she gallops night by night

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Through lovers’ brains, and then they dream of love;

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: On courtiers’ knees, that dream on cur’sies straight;

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: O’er lawyers’ fingers, who straight dream on fees;

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: O’er ladies’ lips, who straight on kisses dream,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Which oft the angry Mab with blisters plagues

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Because their breaths with sweetmeats tainted are.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Sometime she gallops o’er a courtier’s nose,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And then dreams he of smelling out a suit.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And sometime comes she with a tithe-pig’s tail,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Tickling a parson’s nose as he lies asleep;

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Then he dreams of another benefice.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Sometime she driveth o’er a soldier’s neck,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And then dreams he of cutting foreign throats,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Of breaches, ambuscadoes, Spanish blades,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Of healths five fathom deep, and then anon

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And, being thus frighted, swears a prayer or two

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And sleeps again. This is that very Mab

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: That plats the manes of horses in the night

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And bakes the elflocks in foul sluttish hairs,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Which once untangled much misfortune bodes.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: This is the hag, when maids lie on their backs,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: That presses them and learns them first to bear,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Making them women of good carriage.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: This is she—

speaker: ROMEO
text: Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Thou talk’st of nothing.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: True, I talk of dreams,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Which are the children of an idle brain,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Begot of nothing but vain fantasy,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Which is as thin of substance as the air

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And more inconstant than the wind, who woos

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Even now the frozen bosom of the north

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And, being angered, puffs away from thence,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Turning his side to the dew-dropping south.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: This wind you talk of blows us from ourselves.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Supper is done, and we shall come too late.

speaker: ROMEO
text: I fear too early, for my mind misgives

speaker: ROMEO
text: Some consequence yet hanging in the stars

speaker: ROMEO
text: Shall bitterly begin his fearful date

speaker: ROMEO
text: With this night’s revels, and expire the term

speaker: ROMEO
text: Of a despisèd life closed in my breast

speaker: ROMEO
text: By some vile forfeit of untimely death.

speaker: ROMEO
text: But he that hath the steerage of my course

speaker: ROMEO
text: Direct my sail. On, lusty gentlemen.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Strike, drum.
